applyx.title: ApplyX
applyx.subtitle: Local-first job-application tracker
applyx.description: A local-first job-application tracker that reads your own mailbox over IMAP and understands it with a local Ollama model — no cloud LLM, no email forwarding, nothing ever leaving your machine.
applyx.longDescription: A local-first job-application tracker that reads your own mailbox over IMAP and classifies it with a local Ollama model — no OAuth, no email forwarding, no cloud LLM ever seeing your inbox. It follows the full pipeline from applied to offer with real signal, catches soft rejections, recovers hidden employer names from Indeed, and ships a hardened public demo mode. Fully localized in German and English, with per-user encryption at rest.

applyxDetail.heroLeadEmphasis: and nothing ever leaves your machine.

ingest.heading: An email becomes an application
ingest.emailBody: We received your application for the <role>Frontend Engineer</role> role in <location>Berlin</location> and will be in touch soon.

pipeline.heading: Watch it move — with real signal
pipeline.captionApplied: Detected from the confirmation email and slotted in automatically.
pipeline.captionReview: Soft rejections like "we'll keep your profile on file" get caught right here.
pipeline.captionInterview: Scheduling emails move it forward on their own.
pipeline.captionOffer: And if a closed one reopens, ApplyX reopens with it.
pipeline.autoNote: Every move is triggered by a real signal — never dragged by hand.

indeed.heading: Recover the employer Indeed hides
indeed.sub: Indeed's apply-confirmation emails never name the company. So ApplyX looks it up.

local.heading: Local where it counts
local.note: Self-hosted, not a line leaves your machine. The public demo chat runs its reasoning through a swappable provider (OpenRouter, OpenAI-compatible) for performance — same prompts, same validation. Even there, the semantic-search embeddings stay local on Ollama.

jobposting.heading: The posting finds itself
jobposting.sub: From the links in your emails, ApplyX works out the company's applicant system, pulls its open roles, and attaches the matching posting to your application — automatically, while scanning.
jobposting.emailBody: Thanks for your application! You can track its status here:
jobposting.matchNote: Fuzzy-matched on the role title, with (m/f/d) and dashes normalised. Below 0.60 nothing is attached — the wrong posting is worse than none.
jobposting.openingsTitle: Other open roles at the company
jobposting.openingsNote: ATS-first via the public jobs API, otherwise extracted from the career page — cached per company for a day.
jobposting.fallbackTitle: When a login wall is in the way
jobposting.fallbackBody: Pages behind a login (like LinkedIn) are never scraped. When ApplyX detects a login wall, you just paste the URL or the posting text — extraction takes it from there.
jobposting.fallbackTos: JS-heavy postings get rendered by a headless browser (Playwright) only on demand — never in the scan loop, always account- and ToS-safe.

ask.heading: Answers, not filters
ask.sub: Ask your applications in plain language. Instead of setting filters, you get a sentence plus a typed block — and the numbers come from the code, not the model.
ask.q_stat: How many rejections did I get this month?
ask.q_breakdown: How do the outcomes break down?
ask.q_funnel: What does my funnel look like?
ask.a_funnel: Of 8 applications, one reached an offer.
ask.q_detail: Where do things stand with Northwind?
ask.a_detail: Northwind Labs — in interview, last active 3 days ago.
ask.msgText1: The salary range is 65–80k, depending on experience.
ask.msgText2: We'll discuss the exact range in the first call.
ask.retrievalNote: The question is embedded locally; the code pulls the semantically nearest emails by cosine similarity.
ask.step_intentDesc: Count, list, detail or emails — plus any filters from the question.
ask.step_factsDesc: Code filters, counts, groups and retrieves via local embeddings — deterministically.
ask.step_phraseDesc: The model pours the finished facts into one sentence.
ask.step_blockDesc: The server sends a typed block, the frontend renders it.
ask.note: The model never counts — a 7B model miscounts, especially in German. It only classifies the intent and phrases the answer. Everything countable is deterministic code.

assistant.heading: The assistant
assistant.sub: What needs attention right now — and what there is to learn from your own data.
assistant.queueDesc: Reply needed, decide an offer, follow up, or revisit a soft rejection — sorted by urgency.
assistant.digestDesc: A short summary of your funnel plus concrete recommendations — from exact numbers, not a hunch.
assistant.rejectionsDesc: Recurring themes across your rejections — by stack, work mode, and how far you got.
assistant.urgencyDesc: Emails that need a timely reply get flagged — including any stated deadline.
assistant.dedupDesc: Semantic embeddings of “company + role” catch the same application, even worded differently.
assistant.enrichDesc: Seniority, work mode, salary and tech stack — extracted automatically from the emails.

outro.heading: See it for yourself
outro.sub: A public demo, reset on every visit. No account needed.

fingermatch.title: FingerMatch
fingermatch.subtitle: Forensic minutiae matching — real computer vision
fingermatch.description: Two fingerprints, one classical CV pipeline: detect minutiae and singular points, align them, and compute a match score — no black-box model.
fingermatch.longDescription: FingerMatch reproduces the classical dactyloscopy pipeline — Gabor enhancement, skeletonization, crossing-number minutiae, Poincaré singular points and a RANSAC matcher — with OpenCV/scikit-image in a FastAPI backend and a React frontend. A learning and analysis tool, not a court-admissible AFIS.

fingermatchDetail.heroLead: Two prints in, one score out —
fingermatchDetail.heroLeadEmphasis: every step real math, made visible.

enhance.heading: Make it legible first
enhance.sub: Normalize the raw image, estimate the orientation field, sharpen the ridges with Gabor filters, and binarize.

skeleton.heading: Thinned to a single line
skeleton.sub: The ridges are thinned to 1 pixel wide. Short spurs are pruned so they don't become false minutiae.

minutiae.heading: Where ridges end and fork
minutiae.sub: Crossing number: count the eight neighbours of each skeleton pixel. 1 transition = ending, 3 = bifurcation.

singular.heading: The heart of the pattern
singular.sub: Poincaré index: sum the orientation around a loop — +180° is a loop (core), −180° a delta.

match.heading: Do they line up?
match.sub: Two prints are aligned with RANSAC, orientation-consistent minutiae are paired, and a score is computed — shown here in the real analysis cockpit.
match.note: Score 55% — “Likely identical”. A similarity value, not a court-admissible identification.

limits.heading: A score — not an identification
limits.sub: FingerMatch produces a similarity score as a learning and analysis tool.
limits.does1: Detects minutiae and singular points with real CV
limits.does2: Aligns two prints and scores their similarity
limits.doesnt1: Not a court-admissible AFIS, not an identification
limits.classical: Classical pipeline — all four marker types
limits.minutiaenet: Experimental: localizes with a net, classifies classically
limits.detectorNote: The model localizes; the classical logic classifies.

cockpit.heading: The full analysis cockpit
cockpit.sub: Load two prints, filter markers by type, zoom and pan — and compare live.

pitchlab.title: PitchLab
pitchlab.subtitle: Football predictions with a custom model
pitchlab.description: A from-scratch prediction model — Poisson attack/defence, Dixon-Coles, Elo and a Monte-Carlo tournament simulation — on api-football data. No black box: every probability is derived and checked.
pitchlab.longDescription: PitchLab predicts match outcomes and title odds with a model built entirely from scratch: multiplicative Poisson strengths (attack/defence via iterative scaling), the Dixon-Coles low-score correction, Elo, and a 10,000-run Monte-Carlo tournament simulation — on raw api-football data. The model grades its own predictions (calibration, Brier against a naive market) and runs fully offline in seed mode.

pitchlabDetail.heroLeadEmphasis: every probability is derived, and checked.

strengths.heading: Attack and defence, derived
strengths.sub: Iterative scaling estimates each team's attack and defence from match history — nothing hardcoded.

matchmodel.heading: From two λ to a result
matchmodel.sub: Two expected-goal rates give a probability matrix over every scoreline. Dixon-Coles corrects the low scores.
matchmodel.dcNote: corrects the dependence in tight scores (0-0, 1-0, 0-1, 1-1).

montecarlo.heading: 10,000 tournaments, played out
montecarlo.sub: Each run plays out groups and knockouts from the model. Title odds are how often a team wins.

modelcheck.heading: Predictions that grade themselves
modelcheck.sub: We don't just predict — we measure how well. Calibration, Brier score, and a comparison against a naive market.

product.heading: The full analysis cockpit
product.sub: Title race, groups, match analysis and model check — live in demo mode.

outro.heading: A model, not an oracle
outro.sub: PitchLab produces transparent probabilities — not a betting tip, not certainty.
outro.does1: Every number derived from the model
outro.doesnt: Doesn't predict single games with certainty

portfolio.description: A modern, responsive portfolio website built with Next.js 14, featuring smooth animations, 3D elements, and a dark mode. Showcases my projects and experience in web development.
